meta.purpose: Starter corpus of high-frequency waaz (sermon) vocabulary for the Lisaan-ud-Dawat Waaz Companion. Arabic-derived theological, Karbala-narrative, and exhortation register.
meta.verification: Every entry is verified=false on purpose. These are well-aimed starting points, not authority. Confirm each in real waaz use before trusting it, ESPECIALLY any Fatimi/Ismaili community-specific sense. Persian-origin words have no Arabic root (root left empty).
meta.scriptConvention: Arabic naskh. Final feminine -t written as taa marbuta (ۃ) per Lisaan-ud-Dawat convention. Short-vowel diacritics omitted.

entries.meaning: The tenth day of Muharram; the day Imam Husain was martyred at Karbala
entries.note: The peak of Ashara Mubaraka

entries.meaning: Ten; in community use, the ten blessed days of mourning (Ashara Mubaraka)
entries.note: Takes on the specific sense of the Muharram commemoration

entries.meaning: The first month of the Islamic calendar; the month of mourning for Imam Husain
The place in Iraq where Imam Husain and his companions were martyred
Martyrdom; bearing witness with one's life
entries.note: Central theme of Ashara

entries.meaning: The narration of the martyrdom; the account of the killing of Imam Husain
entries.note: Recited during Ashara

entries.meaning: Mourning; ritual lamentation for Imam Husain
The observance of mourning rites for Imam Husain
entries.note: Persian-formed compound on azaa

entries.meaning: The wronged / oppressed one; an epithet of Imam Husain

entries.note: Plural masaa'ib

entries.meaning: Thirst; the thirst endured at Karbala
entries.note: Recurrent motif in Ashara

entries.meaning: Caravan; the caravan of the Ahl al-Bayt
Captive; the captives taken after Karbala
entries.note: Plural asaaraa / usaraa

entries.meaning: Self-sacrifice; ransom of oneself for another
Help; aid; answering the call to support the Imam
Here I am, at your service (response to a call)
entries.note: Said in response to the call of the Imam / of God

entries.meaning: A cry for help; appeal for rescue
The Household of the Prophet Mohammed
The progeny of the Prophet; his pure lineage
Leader; the divinely-guided leader from the Prophet's progeny
The institution and office of the Imam
entries.note: Core Shia/Ismaili doctrine

entries.meaning: Legatee; the appointed successor and trustee
entries.note: Ali as wasi of the Prophet

entries.meaning: Legateeship; the office of the appointed successor
Explicit designation of a successor by the one before him
entries.note: Key term for Imami/Dawat succession

entries.meaning: The summoner; head of the Dawat and leader of the community
entries.note: Al-Dai al-Mutlaq

entries.meaning: The call / mission; the religious establishment of the community
entries.note: As in Lisan al-Dawat, Dawat-e-Hadiyah

entries.meaning: Messengership; the mission of a divine messenger
Master; lord; patron — a title of reverence
entries.note: Used for the Imam / Dai / Ali

entries.meaning: The Five Pure Ones: the Prophet, Ali, Fatima, Hasan, Husain
entries.note: Persian: panj = five

entries.meaning: Gnosis; recognition / knowledge of God and the Imam
Devotion and allegiance to the Imam; his spiritual authority
entries.note: Central pillar; may carry Fatimi-specific depth — verify

entries.meaning: Friend / guardian of God; one invested with walaayat

entries.note: Plural mumineen — common address in waaz

entries.meaning: Religion; faith; the way of life
Inner truth / reality; the esoteric dimension
entries.note: Often paired against shari'at (outer) — Fatimi nuance, verify

entries.meaning: Divine ordainment; that which God ordains
entries.note: Often paired: qaza wa qadar

entries.meaning: The self / soul; the lower self that must be disciplined
The resurrection; the Day of Judgement

entries.note: Role of the Prophet / Imams
Rich theological sense — verify Fatimi usage

entries.meaning: Grace; spiritual emanation / outpouring of bounty
Divine support; confirmation / strengthening from God
entries.note: Often: tayeed of the Imam
Major Ashara theme

entries.meaning: Humility and reverence, especially in worship
Trust; that which is held in trust; trustworthiness
Truthfulness; sincerity in word and deed

entries.note: Often paired with raja (hope)

entries.meaning: Hope; hopeful expectation of God's mercy
Steadfastness; uprightness; perseverance on the path

entries.note: Persian-origin; Arabic equivalent salaat
Persian/Urdu: roza
Persian-origin; Arabic equivalent sawm

entries.meaning: Visitation of a shrine or holy grave
A votive offering, often food, dedicated for blessing
entries.note: Persian-origin

entries.meaning: A vow; a votive offering pledged to God
entries.note: Distinct from nazar (نظر, glance)

entries.meaning: Admonition; the sermon delivered during Ashara
Truth; right; a rightful claim; (al-Haqq) God
Falsehood; that which is false or void
entries.note: Opposite of haqq

entries.meaning: Serenity; contented assurance of the heart
Age; era (also the afternoon prayer time)
entries.note: Context decides which sense
Imam-uz-Zamaan = Imam of the Age
The primordial covenant — verify nuance
Note: distinct from roza (fast)

entries.meaning: Assembly; the morning/evening gathering during Ashara
entries.note: Plural majalis

entries.meaning: The community; the body of believers
A people; a nation or community

entries.note: Phrase of praise
Phrase

entries.meaning: As God has willed (said in admiration / to ward off envy)
entries.note: Phrase
Phrase of glorification

entries.meaning: Peace be upon him (honorific after a prophet/Imam's name)
entries.note: Honorific

entries.meaning: May God bless him and his family (said after the Prophet's name)
entries.note: Honorific; the Bohra form includes 'wa aalihi'
Phrase

entries.meaning: agreement, consensus, or accord (often used in theological context for unanimous doctrine or witness agreement)
entries.note: Common in waaz for discussing scholarly consensus (ijmaa) or unified testimony. May also mean coincidence or happenstance.

entries.meaning: safety, security, trust, covenant; divine protection or assurance
entries.note: Common in waaz when discussing divine protection, spiritual safety, or covenants. Often used in theological context of trusting in God or the Imam's guardianship.

entries.meaning: unveiling, disclosure, revelation—especially of hidden divine truths or spiritual realities
entries.note: Common in Ismaili theology for spiritual insight and esoteric knowledge (ʿilm al-bātin); used in waaz to describe the disclosure of inner meanings of Qur'an and religious teachings.

entries.meaning: the Bestower (of blessings); one of the divine attributes, often referring to God as the source of grace and bounty
entries.note: Common in waaz theology; emphasizes God's role as provider of all good. May appear as part of Quranic epithets.
In waaz context, often refers to worldly governance or political matters, sometimes contrasted with spiritual/divine order. Plural form سياسات (siyāsāt) is also common.
Common in sermon context referring to religious knowledge or divine wisdom; used to describe intellectual and spiritual understanding

entries.meaning: exegesis or interpretation of the Qur'an; detailed explanation of Qur'anic verses and their meanings
entries.note: Standard Islamic term, central to Waaz context where the preacher expounds on Qur'anic passages. Often followed by citation of specific verses.

entries.meaning: kinship, relationship, proximity; spiritual closeness or connection
entries.note: Often used in waaz to describe familial bonds or spiritual intimacy with the Imam or Ahl al-Bayt. May appear in contexts discussing duty to relatives or closeness to the divine.

entries.meaning: calamities, disasters, plagues; trials or afflictions (often spiritual)
entries.note: Common in waaz to refer to worldly misfortunes or spiritual tests. Plural of 'āfah.
Common in waaz discourse, often in phrase 'āl-rozī' (sustenance). Refers to God's provision and care for believers.

entries.meaning: inheritance; succession; legacy; heritage (in theological context, often refers to spiritual or intellectual inheritance, especially of Imami authority or knowledge)
entries.note: Common in Ismaili theology discussing succession of Imamat and transfer of divine knowledge. Can also mean literal inheritance or cultural heritage.

entries.meaning: worn out, exhausted, broken; spiritually depleted or struggling
entries.note: Common in waaz to describe spiritual states or the condition of the soul in hardship. Can mean physically or morally exhausted.

entries.meaning: horizons, spheres, or celestial spheres; used metaphorically in Islamic philosophy and theology for levels of existence or divine manifestation
entries.note: Common in philosophical and mystical contexts within Ismaili thought; may reference the spheres of the cosmos or levels of spiritual understanding
Common in theological discourse; emphasized in Ismaili devotional contexts as spiritual return to the Imam.

entries.meaning: presence; the Imam's presence or divine presence; often used to refer to the Imam (Maulana) directly as a term of reverence
entries.note: Common honorific in Bohra waaz; 'Maulana huzoor' is standard usage for the spiritual leader. Can also denote the assembled congregation in the Imam's presence.

entries.meaning: one who becomes arrogant or presumptuous; one who refuses (to obey)
entries.note: In waaz context, often describes refusal to accept divine guidance or Imam's authority; related to qabar (grave) in some mystical uses, but primary sense is refusal/arrogance

entries.meaning: engineer; skilled craftsman or intellectual architect
entries.note: Direct Arabic borrowing into Lisaan-ud-Dawat; may appear in parables about construction of knowledge or faith
Common in waaz for expressing joy or blessing someone. From Arabic root meaning 'to congratulate.'

entries.meaning: appearance, manifestation; in Ismaili theology, the revealed presence of the Imam or divine guidance
entries.note: key term in Fatimi-Ismaili contexts referring to the manifestation or imamate; often contrasts with concealment (ghayb/satr)

entries.meaning: persons; individuals (plural sense: 'so many souls/people'). Also used in legal or devotional contexts for counting adherents or followers.
entries.note: Alternative grammatical sense; context-dependent whether meaning is spiritual or numerical.

entries.meaning: pious acts; devotional practices; ascetic or righteous deeds
entries.note: In waaz context, often refers to spiritual disciplines or acts of piety performed by the faithful. Plural of taqiyyah in the sense of godliness/piety, distinct from the doctrine of taqiyyah (dissimulation).

entries.meaning: war booty; spiritual gain or blessing (in sermon context, often refers to divine gifts or benefits)
entries.note: Islamic legal term for spoils of war; in waaz used metaphorically for spiritual benefits or blessings granted by Allah or the Imam. Appears in Quranic discourse.

entries.meaning: power, ability, divine omnipotence; often used of God's absolute power and capability
entries.note: Common in theological discourse. In Ismaili context, may reference divine decree and cosmic order. Central to discussions of God's attributes and free will.

entries.meaning: secrets, mysteries; in Ismaili theology, the hidden esoteric meanings of scripture and divine truth
entries.note: Core term in Ismaili waaz: refers to the inner spiritual secrets ('bātin') as opposed to the outer literal meaning ('zāhir'). Frequently invoked in Fatimi and Bohra sermons.
Often used metaphorically in waaz for divine protection or the shadow of the Imam

entries.meaning: regret, repentance; one who regrets or repents
entries.note: Common in Quranic and sermon contexts referring to remorse or turning back from sin. Also used as a name.
Common in sermon context for logical conclusions or consequences of actions/beliefs

entries.meaning: acceptance, pleasure, or contentment (especially God's approval/pleasure); often invoked in contexts of divine favour or the blessing of paradise
entries.note: Common in Quranic and theological contexts. Frequently heard in waaz when discussing acceptance of divine will or God's pleasure. Also the name of the keeper of paradise in Islamic tradition.

entries.meaning: special quality, distinctive characteristic, or particular attribute (often used to describe divine or spiritual properties)
entries.note: Common in theological discourse to denote inherent qualities or specific properties of divine acts or divine guidance (wilāya). May appear in discussions of the Imāms' special attributes.

entries.meaning: shock, blow, impact (physical or emotional); in sermon context often refers to a sudden calamity or trial
entries.note: Common in waaz when describing hardship or unexpected suffering, especially in Karbala narrative contexts

entries.meaning: inhabited, populated, or flourishing; often used in place names or metaphorically for a thriving community
entries.note: Common in Gujarati place names and Persian-influenced contexts. In sermon context may refer to spiritual prosperity or a well-established community.

entries.meaning: al-Samandar: a historical Shi'a/Ismaili site or figure (e.g., al-Samandar, fortress of early Ismaili authority in the Caucasus); may refer to historical or spiritual references in Ismaili tradition
entries.note: If the speaker mentions it as a proper noun or in a historical/theological context, this reading applies; less common in casual waaz but possible in scholarly sermons

entries.meaning: fragments, shards, or broken pieces; figuratively, remnants or debris
entries.note: Common in waaz when describing ruin, destruction, or spiritual dissolution. Quranic resonance (e.g., Quran 105:4 on the Elephant: 'stones of baked clay'). May appear in discussions of sin's destructive effects or the fallen state.

entries.meaning: existent, present, existing; something that exists
entries.note: Common in theological discourse referring to existence or presence, especially divine or metaphysical existence. Often used in contrast with 'ghayr mawjūd' (non-existent).

entries.meaning: servant, attendant; in Ismaili context, often refers to a devoted follower or minister of the Imam
entries.note: Common in waaz; emphasizes spiritual service and devotion. May have specific institutional meaning in Bohra context (e.g., administrative or ceremonial role).

entries.meaning: death, passing away (used of the Prophet or Imams)
entries.note: Common in waaz when referring to the death of the Prophet Muhammad or Imams; carries formal/respectful tone

entries.meaning: proof, evidence, argument; in Ismaili theology, a divinely-appointed authoritative guide or proof of God
entries.note: In waaz context, likely refers to the Imam or a senior spiritual authority as 'the Proof' (Hujjat-ul-Allah). Core Fatimi-Ismaili concept: the Hujjat is the one who can establish truth through decisive proof.

entries.meaning: pleasure, delight, or taste (often used to describe the spiritual pleasure or sweetness of faith)
entries.note: Common in waaz to describe the taste or pleasure of obedience to God, or the spiritual satisfaction found in religious devotion. Can also mean physical pleasure, but sermon context usually implies the higher, spiritual sense.

entries.meaning: one who presents or introduces; a leader or chief; the front rank or vanguard
entries.note: In Dawoodi Bohra context, often refers to a senior community figure or ceremonial leader who introduces proceedings or leads prayers

entries.meaning: oath of allegiance or covenant; in Ismaili context, the solemn pledge of loyalty to the Imam
entries.note: Central to Dawoodi Bohra practice; the formal initiation covenant binding a mumin to the Imam and the Dawat. Often invoked in waaz regarding spiritual commitment and community bonds.
Common theological term emphasizing rarity of divine grace or exceptional spiritual states

entries.meaning: health, wellness; spiritual or physical well-being
entries.note: Common in religious context: health of the soul, spiritual soundness, or divine protection. May appear in dua (prayer) contexts asking for well-being.

entries.meaning: specification, particularization; assigning or determining a specific meaning or application
entries.note: In theological/waaz context, often refers to how a general rule is narrowed or specified to particular cases, or how divine designation applies specifically to chosen ones (esp. in Ismaili theology)

entries.meaning: embalming oils; perfumes and spices used to prepare a body for burial or ceremonial purposes
entries.note: Common in funeral and preparation contexts; may appear in discussions of ritual purification or the Karbala narrative

entries.meaning: cause, reason, motive; in theological context, the instrumental cause or means by which something comes to pass
entries.note: Common in waaz when discussing causality in divine action, Quranic interpretation, or the relationship between human action and divine will

entries.meaning: journey; travel; passage (especially spiritual journey or the journey of the soul)
entries.note: Common in waaz contexts to describe spiritual progression or the journey through life stages. May also refer to the narrative arc of a religious story or the Karbala procession.

entries.meaning: rivers (plural of nahr); often used metaphorically in sermon contexts for flows of divine grace or knowledge
entries.note: Common in Quranic and Fatimi rhetoric; may refer to the rivers of Paradise or streams of sacred wisdom

entries.meaning: imagination; conception; mental image; the faculty of forming an idea of something
entries.note: In Islamic theological context, especially Ismaili thought, tassawur refers to intellectual conception or the formation of an idea in the mind before action (mutakallimun logic). Common in waaz when discussing how understanding ('ilm) precedes practice ('amal).

entries.meaning: rank, station, or degree (especially in hierarchy or spiritual station)
entries.note: Common in waaz when discussing spiritual ranks, angelic stations, or hierarchical positions. Used in Ismaili theology to discuss levels of understanding or proximity to divine truth.

entries.meaning: those who receive or welcome; those who come forward to greet or accept
entries.note: In waaz context, often refers to those who receive divine guidance, baraka (blessing), or the Imam's authority. Can denote welcoming or approaching with honor.

entries.meaning: stage, station, or stopping place; in Ismaili context, often refers to a spiritual station or rank in the hierarchy of faith
entries.note: Common in waaz to denote levels of spiritual understanding or ranks within the community structure. Can also mean 'house' or 'dwelling' in general usage.
Colloquial sense; less likely in formal waaz but possible in discussion of charitable giving.

entries.meaning: divine decree, fate, or predestination; God's measure or proportion of things
entries.note: Core Islamic theological concept; frequently used in waaz to discuss God's will and human agency

entries.meaning: nearness, proximity; closeness to God or the Imam
entries.note: Common in Ismaili theology; refers to spiritual closeness or favour with the divine or Imam. May appear in context of 'qurbat-e-ilahi' (nearness to God).
Less likely in sermon context; faut in the sense of 'death' dominates Dawoodi Bohra waaz

entries.meaning: system, order, arrangement; often refers to religious hierarchy or divine order in waaz context
entries.note: common in sermons discussing cosmic or institutional order; may also reference the Imam's governance structure

entries.meaning: bequest, will, or final testament; in Ismaili context, often refers to the spiritual testament or directive of the Imam
entries.note: Common in waaz when discussing inheritance law, ethical instructions, or Imam's guidance. Fatimi/Ismaili usage emphasizes spiritual authority and succession.

entries.meaning: immersion, submersion; state of being overwhelmed or engrossed (in something)
entries.note: Common in waaz to describe spiritual states—immersion in divine knowledge or love, or being overwhelmed by grief/emotion. Can also mean 'wave' (of the sea) in classical usage.

entries.meaning: sanctity, sacredness, or inviolability; often used for the dignity/respect owed to persons or sacred things
entries.note: Common in waaz when discussing the honor of the Prophet, Imams, or believers; can also mean 'forbidden' (haraam) depending on context

entries.meaning: skilled, expert, proficient; one who is competent or accomplished
entries.note: Common in waaz to describe proficiency in religious knowledge or practice. Can also mean 'dower' (mahr) in different contexts, but the skilled/expert sense is more typical in sermon usage.

entries.meaning: engraving, imprint, inscription; in theological context: divine design or pattern impressed on creation
entries.note: Common in Ismaili philosophy referring to God's signs imprinted in the world; also used for written records or inscriptions

entries.meaning: feeling, sensation, perception; awareness or consciousness (especially of spiritual truths)
entries.note: Common in waaz when discussing emotional or spiritual awareness. Can mean 'sense' or 'intuition' in theological contexts.

entries.meaning: days; epochs; eras (often used in Quranic/theological context for periods of divine trial or significant time)
entries.note: Common in waaz when discussing historical or eschatological periods; also appears in phrases like 'ayyām Allāh' (days of God's manifestation)

entries.meaning: allocation, apportionment, or distribution (of divine gifts, roles, or stations)
entries.note: Common in waaz when discussing how God apportions qualities, knowledge, or spiritual ranks among believers or creation. Related to the concept of divine dispensation.
Common in waaz for explaining divine causality and the reasons behind events or teachings; plural of sabab

entries.meaning: ocean, sea; in mystical/spiritual discourse, often used metaphorically for divine vastness or spiritual depths
entries.note: Urdu/Persian loanword common in Gujarati waaz; typically used poetically or allegorically in religious contexts to evoke the infinite or the divine

entries.meaning: precious, valuable, or rare; applied to something of high worth or excellence
entries.note: Common in waaz to describe virtues, spiritual states, or divine gifts as precious/invaluable

entries.meaning: the pure Imams; the infallible leaders of the Ismaili/Shi'a community, understood to be free from error and spiritual impurity
entries.note: Central Ismaili theology. 'Aimmah' (plural of imam) + 'tahireen' (the pure ones). Refers to the divinely-guided Imams, a cornerstone concept in waaz sermons.

entries.meaning: wisdom; divine wisdom or esoteric knowledge, especially in Ismaili theology
entries.note: In waaz context often refers to spiritual/esoteric wisdom (hikmah) distinct from apparent meaning; central to Fatimi-Ismaili interpretive tradition

entries.meaning: precaution, carefulness, prudence; taking care or safeguarding against error
entries.note: Common in theological discourse; may refer to precautionary principles in religious practice or avoiding doubtful matters

entries.meaning: example, parable, or similitude (often used in Quranic exegesis)
entries.note: Common in waaz; refers to illustrative stories or parables, as in Quranic ayyat (verses) about divine similitudes.

entries.meaning: raised, elevated, exalted; removed or lifted up
entries.note: Common in theological/Quranic contexts for divine elevation or exaltation of status. Often used of the Prophet or the Imams.

entries.meaning: repository, depository; a place or person entrusted with safekeeping of something sacred or spiritual
entries.note: Common in Ismaili theology referring to spiritual trusts, divine knowledge, or sacred texts held in trust. Also used for the Imam as trustee of divine guidance.
Common in sermon context when discussing spiritual repentance or sorrow for sins. Often appears in Quranic and Ismaili ethical discourse.

entries.meaning: the righteous, the pious ones (plural of barr); those who fulfill their covenant with God
entries.note: Common Quranic term. In waaz context, refers to the spiritually elevated and morally upright believers, often contrasted with sinners. Frequently appears in discussion of moral hierarchy and divine reward.

entries.meaning: bound, tied; connected; established (as in covenant)
entries.note: less likely but possible if vowel heard as short 'a'; relates to binding or establishing bonds

entries.meaning: majesty, dignity, awe-inspiring presence or authority (especially of God or spiritual leaders)
entries.note: Common in waaz; describes the commanding, awesome presence that inspires reverence. Often applied to Allah's majesty or the Imam's spiritual authority.

entries.meaning: severity, intensity, hardship, or distress (esp. spiritual/temporal trials)
entries.note: Common in waaz for describing trials, afflictions, or the intensity of faith tests; Quranic usage

entries.meaning: in agreement; conforming; matching; consistent with
entries.note: Common in theological discourse to describe doctrinal alignment or textual correspondence. Used to affirm that one position accords with another (scripture, precedent, logic).

entries.meaning: to follow, emulate, or imitate (especially the Prophet or Imam)
entries.note: Common in waaz: believers are urged to follow the Imam's example and guidance. Related to 'qudwah' (exemplar).

entries.meaning: summary; essence; the main point or crux of a matter
entries.note: Common in waaz to introduce or conclude a key theological point. Standard Arabic term.

entries.meaning: chosen, selected; (especially) the one chosen by God; referring to someone or something of divine selection or election
entries.note: Common in Ismaili theological context, particularly for Imams and divine appointees. Can also mean 'consulted' (past participle of استخار). In waaz, likely refers to divine selection or appointment.

entries.meaning: commerce, trade, business; metaphorically used for spiritual exchange or transaction in theological contexts
entries.note: Common Quranic term (e.g., Surah Fatir 29). In waaz context, often used figuratively for spiritual dealings or covenant with Imam.

entries.meaning: table, dining table; feast; sustenance (often with spiritual or covenant connotations)
entries.note: In Quranic and sermon contexts, may refer to the Table sent down to the disciples (Surah al-Mā'idah). In waaz, often evokes covenant meal or spiritual provision.

entries.meaning: dragon; metaphorical figure of evil, oppression, or Satan in Islamic literature
entries.note: Persian loanword used in theological and narrative contexts; appears in Karbala and Quranic allegory. In waaz, may reference spiritual or temporal tyranny.
Persian-origin Urdu/Gujarati term; possible if initial was heard as 'kh' rather than 'q'

entries.meaning: arrogance, pride, haughtiness; one of the major sins condemned in Islamic teaching
entries.note: Quranic term appearing frequently in waaz discourse on spiritual vices. Often discussed in contrast to humility and submission to Allah.

entries.meaning: skirt, hem, or shelter/protection (fig. the refuge or protection of someone)
entries.note: Common in waaz for metaphorical 'taking shelter' in divine protection or a leader's guidance. Often used: 'daaman mein lena' (to take under one's protection).
Used in Urdu/Gujarati waaz idiom to describe divine attributes or exceptional qualities; often applied to prophets or Imams

entries.meaning: administrator, manager, or one who arranges/plans; in theological context, often refers to divine management of affairs
entries.note: Common in waaz discourse discussing divine providence (tadbīr) or governance. May appear in discussions of cosmic order or God's careful orchestration of events.

entries.meaning: retaliation in kind; the law of equivalent retaliation (lex talionis), especially 'an eye for an eye'
entries.note: Quranic term (2:178, 5:45 etc.) used in sermons discussing divine justice and punishment; foundational to Islamic legal theory

entries.meaning: poor, miserable, wretched; often used in Quranic context to denote spiritual or material poverty
entries.note: Common in waaz when discussing human weakness, reliance on God, or the condition of the spiritually destitute. Quranic usage.

entries.meaning: conversation, dialogue, discourse; often used in waaz to mean spiritual dialogue or exchange of knowledge
entries.note: Common in sermon context for discussions between imam and followers, or theological exchanges; close to Persian/Urdu guftagū
More emphatic form; common in Quranic-style exegesis in sermons

entries.meaning: inquiry, questioning; seeking clarification or information
entries.note: Common in sermon context for asking about religious matters or seeking understanding. May also refer to drawing out or extracting knowledge.

entries.meaning: immersion in or absorption by; engrossment; state of being overwhelmed or submerged
entries.note: Used in waaz to describe spiritual states—e.g., being immersed in divine love or truth. Related to gharq (drowning). Common in Ismaili theological discourse.

entries.meaning: miracles or wondrous deeds, especially those performed by saints or the spiritually elevated (in Ismaili context, often referring to the extraordinary spiritual powers of the Imams)
entries.note: Common in waaz; plural of kirāmat (singular). In Ismaili theology, often used to describe the supernatural knowledge and protection granted by the Imam. Distinct from mu'jizāt (Quranic miracles).

entries.meaning: forgiveness, pardon, or a gift/alms given as an act of grace
entries.note: In sermon context, often used for divine forgiveness or mercy (bakhshish from Allah); can also mean charitable gift. Common in Quranic and devotional language.
Virtue emphasized in Islamic ethics and Ismaili teachings; often used in sermons to describe moral excellence and the willingness to give and forgive.

entries.meaning: request, demand, or seeking; often used in theological contexts for spiritual striving or pursuit of knowledge
entries.note: Common in waaz for 'seeking' divine guidance or knowledge; can also mean 'request' in general sense

entries.meaning: island; peninsula; in Islamic theological context, can denote a place of refuge or isolation
entries.note: Common in classical Islamic geography and exegesis; may appear in narratives about prophets or saints seeking refuge

entries.meaning: rule; governance; authority (esp. divine or of the Imam)
entries.note: Common in waaz when discussing Imamate, divine authority, or governance. Can refer to the Imam's spiritual rulership.

entries.meaning: correct, right, proper; straightforward or upright (especially in moral/doctrinal sense)
entries.note: Common in waaz context to affirm correct belief or practice; may carry moral weight beyond mere 'correct'

entries.meaning: restriction, limitation, constraint; condition or rule imposed
entries.note: Common in waaz to describe divine or legal restrictions (sharīʿah-based limitations). Gujarati-Persian loanword.

entries.meaning: The Creator; God as the eternal originator of all existence
entries.note: One of the 99 Names of Allah; frequently invoked in waaz to emphasize divine omnipotence and the dependence of creation on God's will

entries.meaning: the straight path; often invoked in Quranic sense as the righteous way to God
entries.note: Homonymic with above in pronunciation; this form emphasizes spiritual/theological path rather than biographical narrative

entries.meaning: preceding, previous, prior; one who comes before
entries.note: common in theological contexts meaning 'predecessor' or 'what came before'; can refer to prior imams or prior knowledge in Ismaili thought

entries.meaning: trust in God; reliance upon divine will and providence
entries.note: Core Islamic concept; in waaz context emphasizes surrendering outcomes to Allah after exertion of effort

entries.meaning: upbringing, education, spiritual cultivation and training in religious discipline
entries.note: Common in Ismaili waaz context, especially regarding spiritual education, moral formation, and cultivation of followers by the Imam or community leaders

entries.meaning: longing, yearning, intense desire (often for spiritual closeness or reunion with the Imām)
entries.note: Common in waaz to describe the spiritual yearning of believers, especially in Karbala narratives and devotional contexts

entries.meaning: profitable, gaining, victorious (esp. of faith or righteousness)
entries.note: Common in waaz to describe spiritual triumph or the profitable state of a believer; feminine form of rābeḥ.

entries.meaning: attributes, qualities (especially of God; divine attributes in Islamic theology)
entries.note: In waaz context, refers to God's attributes or qualities. Core theological term in Ismaili exegesis.
Common in waaz for describing religious duties or logical necessities. Often used in phrases like 'lazim hai' (it is necessary).

entries.meaning: grace, favour, or divine bestowal; often used in waaz to refer to Allah's special blessing or the Imam's favour toward the community
entries.note: Common in Ismaili/Fatimi theology; can denote both divine grace and the Imam's patronage. Spelled ʿinayah in Classical Arabic, but Lisaan-ud-Dawat uses inayat.

entries.meaning: choice, free will, discretion; authority or decision-making power
entries.note: In theological context, often refers to free will as a doctrine in Islamic philosophy, or the authority/prerogative of the Imam. Common in waaz discourse on divine will and human agency.

entries.meaning: to consume, to take, to handle; in sermon context often refers to consuming food (especially in relation to halal/haram) or partaking in a practice or tradition
entries.note: Common in Lisaan-ud-Dawat sermons when discussing eating practices, consumption of permissible things, or metaphorically taking up or undertaking something

entries.meaning: great, mighty, supreme; often used to describe Allah's attributes or majesty
entries.note: Common in Quranic and theological contexts; frequently appears in phrases like 'Allah al-azeem' (the Mighty God)

entries.meaning: most, majority, most of the time
entries.note: Common in waaz for emphasizing frequency or prevalence of a quality or action

entries.meaning: brought up, reared, trained; cultivated (spiritual growth)
entries.note: used in waaz context for spiritual nurturing or development, as in 'Allah rabbat' (God trained/shaped)

entries.meaning: one who advances; a forerunner or herald; one who comes forward
entries.note: In waaz context, often refers to those who precede or herald a greater figure (e.g., forerunners of Imam). May also mean 'one who employs or makes use of' depending on context.
Quranic term; in waaz context refers to divine command or the Imam's authority to command the faithful

entries.meaning: provision, sustenance, livelihood; divine bounty bestowed by God
entries.note: Common in Quranic and sermon contexts; refers to material and spiritual provision from Allah. Fundamental concept in Islamic theology and ethics.

entries.meaning: health, wellness, deliverance from illness or hardship; often used in du'a (prayer) contexts
entries.note: Common in waaz when invoking divine protection or blessing; may appear in phrases like 'afiyat de haal' (state of wellness)

entries.meaning: protection, safeguarding, or guardianship; often used for divine protection over believers or the community
entries.note: Common in waaz for spiritual protection and preservation. Also used for custody or trusteeship of sacred trusts.

entries.meaning: rider; horseman; one who rides (often used metaphorically in religious contexts for spiritual progression)
entries.note: Common in sermon narrative, especially in Karbala contexts (e.g., riders/warriors). Can also mean 'to ride' or refer to spiritual elevation.

entries.meaning: desires, wishes, or inclinations (pl. of raghbah)
entries.note: Common in waaz contexts for discussing worldly desires or spiritual inclinations; often appears in moral/ethical discussions about attachment to this world versus the hereafter.

entries.meaning: the pious, the God-fearing ones; those who practice taqwa (God-consciousness and righteousness)
entries.note: Quranic term, frequent in waaz. Refers to believers who maintain awareness of God and act righteously. Core Islamic virtue especially emphasized in Ismaili theology.

entries.meaning: law, rule, statute; in theological context: divine ordinance or established principle
entries.note: Common in waaz when discussing Islamic jurisprudence (fiqh) or divine law (shari'ah). May appear in phrases about established religious practice.

entries.meaning: established, fixed, permanent; proven or confirmed
entries.note: Common in theological discourse for immutable divine attributes or established truths (Quranic usage). May appear in phrases like 'haqq-e sābit' (established truth).

entries.meaning: protected, preserved, safeguarded (often used in theological context for divine protection or preserved knowledge)
entries.note: Common in waaz referring to divine guardianship, protected/hidden knowledge (ʿilm), or the Imam's protection of the community. May also appear as 'mehfoozi' (adj. form).

entries.meaning: heedlessness, neglect, inattention; spiritual indifference or being unmindful of God
entries.note: Common in waaz to warn against ghaflat as a state of spiritual danger; often paired with concepts of remembrance (dhikr) and awareness.

entries.meaning: souls; selves (plural of nafs). In sermon context: the spiritual essence or ego-self that must be disciplined or purified.
entries.note: Common in Quranic and Fatimi theology. 'Nafs' refers to the self/ego that leads to desire and passion; purification of the nafs is a spiritual goal.

entries.meaning: biography, life story, or conduct; often used to refer to the Prophet Muhammad's life narrative or spiritual path
entries.note: Common in waaz context for discussing prophetic narratives and exemplary lives; can also mean 'way' or 'path' in spiritual sense

entries.meaning: glorification of God; the phrase 'Allāhu akbar' (God is greatest); laudation
entries.note: Common in waaz; refers to expressions of divine praise, especially 'Allāhu akbar' or the act of proclaiming God's greatness. Also used for ceremonial acclamations during Ashura commemorations.

entries.meaning: confrontations, oppositions, or face-to-face encounters; often used for theological or spiritual confrontations
entries.note: In waaz context, may refer to spiritual opposition or confrontation between believers and disbelievers, or between divine truth and falsehood. Could also mean 'locking horns' or standing opposite. Root primarily conveys 'standing opposite/facing.'

entries.meaning: Remains; traces, relics, or surviving signs of something.
entries.note: Plural of athar; in waaz often refers to relics or traces left by the Awliya.
Persian-origin; describes a heart heavy with grief, common in marsiya context.
Persian-origin.

entries.meaning: Beginning; the start or commencement of something.
entries.note: Persian-origin.
Persian-origin; often metaphorical for radiant guidance in poetry.
Persian-origin; sense ranges from awareness to foretelling.

entries.meaning: Front runner; one who leads or goes ahead, a forerunner.
entries.note: Persian/Urdu-origin.
Plural of bab; figuratively chapters or means of access.

entries.meaning: Impact; influence, effect, or impression left by something.
entries.note: From athar (أثر); distinct from ʿaṣr/other homographs.

entries.meaning: Reward; recompense for a good deed.
entries.note: Spiritual reward from Allah for righteous acts.

entries.meaning: Sunday (the first day of the week).
entries.note: Yawm al-ahad; also a divine name meaning 'the One'.
In Ismaili da'wat, Asās is also a title for Amir al-Mumineen Ali (AS) as the foundation of ta'wil.
Often the companions of the Prophet or of Imam Husain at Karbala.
From أصl; distinct from 'asal (honey).
Qur'anic phrase describing reward multiplied abundantly.
The enemies of the Ahl al-Bayt in waaz/marsiya.

entries.meaning: A bedouin; an Arab from a rural or desert area.
entries.note: Refers to a desert-dwelling Arab, distinct from town Arabs.

entries.meaning: Deed; action or work, especially righteous action.
entries.note: From عمل; distinct from amal (hope). Good deeds (a'mal) earn reward.
From أمل; distinct from 'amal (deed).

entries.meaning: Rich; also a leader, commander, or prince.
entries.note: Amir as leader/commander (e.g. Amir al-Mumineen); colloquially also 'wealthy'.

entries.meaning: End result; outcome, conclusion, or fulfillment.
entries.note: Persian-origin.

entries.meaning: Affection; warmth, familiarity, and companionable fondness.
entries.note: Spelling reflects sukoon; from the root for intimacy/anas.

entries.meaning: Body parts; limbs or members (plural of 'udw).
entries.note: Heard form 'angwa' is a colloquial rendering of a'za'.
The divine lights of the Imams/Ahl al-Bayt (al-anwar).
Lines of poetry, e.g. in qasida or marsiya.
E.g. al-Hajar al-Aswad, the Black Stone.

entries.meaning: World; the universe or a realm of existence.
entries.note: From عالم; distinct from 'alam (flag) and alam (pain).
From عسl; distinct from asl (root/origin).
From ألم; distinct from 'alam (flag) and 'alam (world).
From علم; the 'alam of Hazrat Abbas (AS) is central to Karbala remembrance.

entries.meaning: At any cost; in any case, no matter what.
entries.note: Set Arabic phrase used to mean 'come what may'.
Often the children of Karbala (atfal) in marsiya.
E.g. the staff of Musa (AS).
Persian-origin; the embrace/lap, e.g. a mother holding a child.
Persian-origin; tears shed, especially in mourning/marsiya.

entries.meaning: Eternal; without beginning, pre-existing for all time.
entries.note: Used of God and divine attributes as having no beginning.
Arabic ʿaql 'intellect' + Persian suffix -mand.

entries.meaning: Vehicle; a carrier or conveyance for loads.
entries.note: Persian: bār 'load' + kash 'carrier'.
Persian/Urdu adverb.

entries.meaning: To stay away from; to refrain or abstain from.
entries.note: Persian bāz 'back/away' + Gujarati verb rehvu 'to remain'.
Persian-origin.

entries.meaning: Inner; hidden; the esoteric or concealed dimension.
entries.note: Central Ismaili/Fatimi concept paired with ẓāhir (outer); refers to the inner meaning of revelation.
Persian/Urdu-origin.
Persian/Urdu; e.g. bajā lāvvu 'to carry out properly'.

entries.meaning: Rug; mat; bedding spread on the floor.
